Q Text: Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.

Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
Answer Match: read1(x); read1(y); write1(x); read2(x); write2(x); commit2(x); write1(x); commit1(x,y)

Answer: T1: Serializable and T2 Read Uncommitted

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT     Scientist.name,      COUNT(Teaches.student) AS student_count FROM      Scientist, Teaches WHERE      Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor GROUP BY      Scientist.id, Scientist.name ORDER BY      student_count DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT* FROM      Scientist WHERE      Scientist.id IN (         SELECT Teaches.advisor         FROM Teaches         WHERE Teaches.student = (             SELECT id             FROM Scientist             WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'         )     );

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE Scientist.id IN ( SELECT Teaches.student         FROM Teaches         WHERE Teaches.advisor = (             SELECT id             FROM Scientist             WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'         ) );

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT  T1.advisor, T2.student FROM  Teaches T1, Teaches T2 WHERE T1.student = T2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (     SELECT advisor, student     FROM Teaches     UNION ALL     SELECT A.advisor, T.student     FROM Ancestors A     JOIN Teaches T ON A.student = T.advisor ) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (     SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year     FROM Scientist s     WHERE s.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'       UNION ALL       SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year     FROM Scientist s, Teaches t, Descendants d     WHERE t.advisor = d.id       AND t.student = s.id ) SELECT DISTINCT id, name, university, year FROM Descendants WHERE name != 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (     SELECT advisor, student     FROM Teaches     UNION ALL     SELECT A.advisor, T.student     FROM Ancestors A     JOIN Teaches T ON A.student = T.advisor ) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (     SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year     FROM Scientist S     WHERE S.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'     UNION ALL     SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year     FROM Descendants D     JOIN Teaches T ON D.id = T.advisor     JOIN Scientist S ON T.student = S.id ) SELECT DISTINCT id, name, university, year FROM Descendants WHERE name != 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.
Answer Match: read1(x); read1(y); read2(x); write2(x); write1(x); write1(y)

Q Text: Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
Answer Match: read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y); read2(x);  commit1(x,y); write2(x); commit2(x);

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S WHERE S.university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S WHERE S.university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT S.name, COUNT(T.student) FROM Scientist S JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.advisor GROUP BY S.name ORDER BY COUNT(T.student) DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.advisor WHERE T.student = (     SELECT id     FROM Scientist     WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' );

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.student WHERE T.advisor = (     SELECT id     FROM Scientist     WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' );

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT T1.advisor AS grandparent, T2.student AS grandchild FROM Teaches T1 JOIN Teaches T2 ON T1.student = T2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS  (     SELECT T.advisor, T.student     FROM Teaches T     UNION     SELECT A.advisor, T.student     FROM Ancestors A     JOIN Teaches T ON A.student = T.advisor ) SELECT * FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS  (     SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year     FROM Scientist S     JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.student     WHERE T.advisor = (         SELECT id         FROM Scientist         WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'     )     UNION     SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year     FROM Scientist S     JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.student     JOIN Descendants D ON T.advisor = D.id ) SELECT * FROM Descendants;

Answer: SELECT      s.name AS scientist_name,     COUNT(t.student) AS num_students FROM      Scientist s LEFT JOIN      Teaches t ON s.id = t.advisor GROUP BY      s.id, s.name ORDER BY      num_students DESC;

Answer: SELECT      s.id,      s.name,      s.university,      s.year FROM      Scientist AS s JOIN      Teaches AS t ON s.id = t.advisor WHERE      t.student = (         SELECT id          FROM Scientist          WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'     );

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (     -- Base case: direct advisor-student relationships     SELECT          advisor,          student     FROM          Teaches     UNION     -- Recursive case: find ancestors by linking to the base case     SELECT          a.advisor,          t.student     FROM          Ancestors AS a     JOIN          Teaches AS t ON a.student = t.advisor ) SELECT      advisor,      student FROM      Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (     -- Base case: find direct students of 'Desiderius Erasmus'     SELECT          t.student AS id     FROM          Teaches t     JOIN          Scientist s ON t.advisor = s.id     WHERE          s.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'     UNION     -- Recursive case: find students of students     SELECT          t.student     FROM          Descendants d     JOIN          Teaches t ON d.id = t.advisor ) SELECT      s.id,      s.name,      s.university,      s.year FROM      Descendants d JOIN      Scientist s ON d.id = s.id;

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year  FROM Scientist  WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT S.name AS scientist_name, COUNT(T.student) AS student_count  FROM Scientist S  LEFT JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.advisor  GROUP BY S.id, S.name  ORDER BY student_count DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.advisor WHERE T.student = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year  FROM Scientist S  JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.student  WHERE T.advisor = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT T1.advisor AS grandparent, T2.student AS grandchild FROM Teaches T1 JOIN Teaches T2 ON T1.student = T2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors(advisor, student) AS (       SELECT advisor, student       FROM Teaches       UNION       SELECT A.advisor, T.student       FROM Ancestors A       JOIN Teaches T ON A.student = T.advisor ) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants(id, name, university, year) AS (     SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year     FROM Scientist S     JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.student     WHERE T.advisor = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus')     UNION     SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year     FROM Scientist S     JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.student     JOIN Descendants D ON T.advisor = D.id ) SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Descendants;

Q Text: Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.
Answer Match: read1(x); read1(y); read2(x); write1(x); write2(x); write1(y).

Q Text: Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
Answer Match: read1(x);  read1(y); read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x); write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y);

Answer: T1: Read Committed and T2 Read Committed

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT Scientist.name, COUNT(Teaches.student) FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor GROUP BY Scientist.name HAVING COUNT(Teaches.student) ORDER BY COUNT(Teaches.student) DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT A.id, A.name, A.university, A.year FROM Scientist A, Teaches, Scientist S WHERE A.id=Teaches.advisor AND Teaches.student = S.id AND S.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist A, Teaches, Scientist S WHERE A.id=Teaches.advisor AND Teaches.student = S.id AND A.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT S.name, G.name FROM Scientist S, Scientist A, Scientist G, Teaches T, Teaches D WHERE S.id=T.student AND A.id=T.advisor AND A.id=D.student AND G.id=D.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Parent(name,id) AS( SELECT A.name, S.id FROM Scientist S, Scientist A, Teaches T WHERE S.id=T.student AND A.id=T.advisor UNION SELECT Parent.name, Parent.id FROM Parent, Scientist G, Teaches D WHERE Parent.id=D.student AND G.id=D.advisor ) SELECT DISTINCT Scientist.name, Parent.name FROM Scientist, Parent, Teaches WHERE (Parent.id=Scientist.id) ;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Parent(name,id, university, year) AS( SELECT S.name, S.id, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S, Teaches T, Scientist A WHERE S.id=T.student AND A.id=T.Advisor AND A.name='Desiderius Erasmus' UNION SELECT Q.name, Q.id, Q.university, Q.year FROM Scientist Q, Teaches P, Scientist B, Parent WHERE Q.id=P.student AND B.id=P.Advisor AND B.name=Parent.name ) SELECT DISTINCT * FROM Parent;

Answer: SELECT s.name, COUNT(t.student) AS student_count FROM Scientist s JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.advisor GROUP BY s.name ORDER BY student_count DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s.name, COUNT(t.student) AS student_count FROM Scientist s JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.advisor GROUP BY s.name ORDER BY student_count DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientist s JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.student WHERE t.advisor = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT t1.advisor AS grandparent, t2.student AS grandchild FROM Teaches t1 JOIN Teaches t2 ON t1.student = t2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (     -- Base case: Direct advisor-student relationships     SELECT advisor, student     FROM Teaches     UNION ALL     -- Recursive case: Find indirect advisor-student relationships (ancestor-descendant)     SELECT t1.advisor, t2.student     FROM Teaches t1     JOIN Ancestors t2 ON t1.student = t2.advisor ) SELECT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (     -- Base case: Find all students of Desiderius Erasmus     SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year, t.student     FROM Scientist s     JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.advisor     WHERE s.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'          UNION ALL          -- Recursive case: Find descendants of already found students     SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year, t.student     FROM Scientist s     JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.advisor     JOIN Descendants d ON t.student = d.student ) SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Descendants;

Q Text: Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Answer Match: read2(x); read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(y); write1(x); write2(x);

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT student) AS total, name FROM Scientist JOIN Teaches ON Teaches.advisor = Scientist.id GROUP BY  Scientist.id, name ORDER BY total DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT * FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE id = advisor AND student = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors(advisor, student, level) AS  (     SELECT T.advisor, T.student, 1 AS level     FROM Teaches T     UNION ALL     SELECT T.advisor, A.student, A.level + 1     FROM Teaches T     JOIN Ancestors A ON T.student = A.advisor     WHERE A.level < 2  ) SELECT * FROM Ancestors A1 WHERE A1.level = 2;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors(advisor, student) AS (     SELECT T.advisor, T.student     FROM Teaches T     UNION     SELECT T.advisor, A.student     FROM Teaches T     JOIN Ancestors A ON T.student = A.advisor ) SELECT * FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE ErasmusDescendants AS (     SELECT *     FROM Scientist S     WHERE S.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'     UNION     SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year     FROM Scientist S     JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.student     JOIN ErasmusDescendants E ON E.id = T.advisor ) SELECT id, name, university, year FROM ErasmusDescendants WHERE name <> 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE AdvisorAncestors(id) AS ( SELECT A.advisor AS id FROM Teaches A   UNION   SELECT A.advisor AS id FROM AdvisorAncestors B, Teaches A WHERE B.id = A.student AND NOT A.advisor IS NULL ) SELECT * FROM AdvisorAncestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE AdvisorAncestors(id) AS ( SELECT A.student AS id FROM Teaches A   UNION   SELECT A.student AS id FROM AdvisorAncestors B, Teaches A WHERE B.id = A.advisor AND NOT A.student IS NULL ) SELECT * FROM AdvisorAncestors;

Q Text: Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.
Answer Match: read1(x); read1(y); read2(x); write1(x); write1(y); write2(x)

Q Text: Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
Answer Match: read1(x); read1(y); write1(x); write1(y);read2(x); write2(x); commit2(x); commit1(x,y)

Answer: T1 Read Committed; T2 Read Uncommitted

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: Select S.name, S.id, COUNT (*) FROM Scientist S, Teaches T WHERE S.id = T.advisor GROUP BY S.id, S.name ORDER BY COUNT (*) DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S, Teaches T WHERE S.id = T.advisor AND T.student IN ( SELECT S.id FROM Scientist S WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S, Teaches T WHERE S.id = T.student AND T.advisor IN ( SELECT S.id FROM Scientist S WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT T1.advisor, T2.student FROM Teaches T1, Teaches T2 WHERE T1.student = T2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS ( SELECT T.advisor, T.student FROM Teaches T UNION SELECT A.advisor, T.student FROM Ancestors A, Teaches T WHERE A.student = T.advisor AND T.student <> A.advisor  ) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS ( SELECT T.advisor, T.student FROM Teaches T WHERE T.advisor IN ( SELECT S.id FROM Scientist S WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus') UNION SELECT A.advisor, T.student FROM Ancestors A, Teaches T WHERE A.student = T.advisor AND T.student <> A.advisor  ) SELECT DISTINCT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Ancestors A, Scientist S WHERE A.student = S.id;

Answer: WITH RECURSIVE AncestorRelation AS (     SELECT advisor, student     FROM Teaches     UNION       SELECT ar.advisor, t.student     FROM AncestorRelation ar     JOIN Teaches t ON ar.student = t.advisor ) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM AncestorRelation;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (     SELECT t.student, s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year     FROM Teaches t     JOIN Scientist s ON t.student = s.id     WHERE t.advisor = (         SELECT id         FROM Scientist         WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'     )     UNION     SELECT t.student, s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year     FROM Teaches t     JOIN Scientist s ON t.student = s.id     JOIN Descendants d ON t.advisor = d.id ) SELECT DISTINCT id, name, university, year FROM Descendants;

Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (     SELECT advisor, student     FROM Teaches     UNION     SELECT A.advisor, T.student     FROM Ancestors A, Teaches T     WHERE A.student = T.advisor ) SELECT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE Scientist.id = Teaches.student AND Teaches.advisor = (SELECT Scientist.id FROM Scientist WHERE Scientist.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT Teaches.advisor, Teaches2.student FROM Teaches, Teaches AS Teaches2 WHERE Teaches.student = Teaches2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors(advisor, student) AS ( -- advisor, student pairs from teaches table SELECT advisor, student FROM Teaches   UNION   -- the students of the students -- by joining the current Ancestors table with the teaches table SELECT Ancestors.advisor, Teaches.student FROM Ancestors, Teaches WHERE Ancestors.student = Teaches.advisor)   SELECT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors(advisor, student) AS ( -- advisor, student pairs from teaches table where Erasmus is teacher SELECT advisor, student FROM Teaches, Scientist WHERE Teaches.Advisor = Scientist.id AND Scientist.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'   UNION   -- the students of the students  -- by joining the current Ancestors table with the teaches table SELECT Ancestors.advisor, Teaches.student FROM Ancestors, Teaches WHERE Ancestors.student = Teaches.advisor)   SELECT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year FROM Scientist, Ancestors WHERE Scientist.id = Ancestors.student;

Q Text: Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.
Answer Match: T1 = read1(x); read2(x); write1(x); read1(y) T2 = write2(x); write1(y)

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT name, COUNT(student) FROM Scientist JOIN Teaches ON id=advisor GROUP BY name ORDER BY COUNT(student) DESC;

Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestordescendant(adid, stid) AS  (  SELECT advisor AS adid, student AS stid FROM Teaches T UNION  SELECT T.advisor AS adid, A.stid FROM Ancestordescendant A, Teaches T WHERE A.adid == T.student )  SELECT * FROM Ancestordescendant;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)

Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
Answer Match: **Execution Order**:   `T1: read1(x), T1: write1(x), T2: read2(x), T2: write2(x), T2: commit2(x), T1: write1(y), T1: commit1(x, y)`

Q Text: We want to prevent dirty reads by transaction T1, and we want to put minimal constraints on the concurrent execution of transactions T1 and T2. What isolation level(s) do we need for T1 and/or T2?
Answer: T1: Read Committed and T2 Read Committed

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT S.name, COUNT(T.student) AS number_of_students FROM Scientist S LEFT JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.advisor GROUP BY S.id, S.name ORDER BY number_of_students DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.advisor WHERE T.student = (     SELECT id     FROM Scientist     WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' );

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.student WHERE T.advisor = (     SELECT id     FROM Scientist     WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' );

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT T1.advisor AS grandparent, T2.student AS grandchild FROM Teaches T1 JOIN Teaches T2 ON T1.student = T2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (     SELECT advisor, student     FROM Teaches       UNION ALL       SELECT A.advisor, T.student     FROM Ancestors A     JOIN Teaches T ON A.student = T.advisor ) SELECT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (     SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year     FROM Scientist S     JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.student     WHERE T.advisor = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus')       UNION ALL       SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year     FROM Scientist S     JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.student     JOIN Descendants D ON T.advisor = D.id ) SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Descendants;

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (     -- Base case: Select direct advisors for each student     SELECT advisor, student     FROM Teaches     WHERE advisor IS NOT NULL          UNION ALL          -- Recursive case: Find the advisors of the current advisors (i.e., find ancestors)     SELECT T1.advisor, T2.student     FROM Teaches T1     JOIN Ancestors T2 ON T1.student = T2.advisor ) SELECT advisor, student FROM Ancestors ORDER BY advisor, student;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (     -- Base case: Find all direct students of 'Desiderius Erasmus'     SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year     FROM Scientist S     WHERE S.id IN (         SELECT T.student         FROM Teaches T         WHERE T.advisor = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus')     )          UNION ALL          -- Recursive case: Find students of the current students (i.e., descendants)     SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year     FROM Scientist S     JOIN Descendants D ON S.id = D.id     JOIN Teaches T ON T.advisor = D.id ) SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Descendants ORDER BY id;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (       SELECT advisor, student     FROM Teaches     UNION       SELECT t1.advisor, t2.student     FROM Teaches t1     JOIN Ancestors a ON t1.student = a.advisor     JOIN Teaches t2 ON a.student = t2.advisor ) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)

Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.

Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
Answer Match: read1(x); read1(y); write1(x); write1(y); read2(x); write2(x); commit2(x)

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university='Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT name, COUNT(student) FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE Scientist.id=Teaches.advisor GROUP BY name ORDER BY COUNT(st udent) desc;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year FROM Scientist, Teaches, Teaches AS Teaches2, Scientist AS Scientist2 WHERE Scientist.id=Teaches.advisor AND Scientist2.id=Teaches2.student AND Teaches.student=Teaches2.student AND Scientist2.name='Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT Scientist2.id, Scientist2.name, Scientist2.university, Scientist2.year FROM Scientist, Teaches, Teaches AS Teaches2, Scientist AS Scientist2 WHERE Scientist.id=Teaches.advisor AND Scientist2.id=Teaches2.student AND Teaches.student=Teaches2.student AND Scientist.name='Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT A.advisor, B.student FROM teaches A, teaches B WHERE A.student = B.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE ancestor(advisor, student) AS (SELECT advisor, student FROM Teaches UNION SELECT Teaches.advisor, ancestor.student FROM ancestor, Teaches WHERE Teaches.student = ancestor.advisor) SELECT * FROM ancestor;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE ancestor(advisor, student) AS (SELECT advisor, student FROM Teaches UNION SELECT Teaches.advisor, ancestor.student FROM ancestor, Teaches WHERE Teaches.student = ancestor.advisor) SELECT Scientist2.id, Scientist2.name, Scientist2.university, Scientist2.year FROM Scientist Scientist1, Scientist Scientist2, ancestor WHERE Scientist1.id = ancestor.advisor AND Scientist1.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' AND Scientist2.id = ancestor.student;

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT S.name, COUNT(T.student) AS student_count FROM Scientist S, Teaches T WHERE S.id = T.advisor GROUP BY S.name ORDER BY student_count DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year FROM Scientist, Teaches, Scientist AS Student WHERE Student.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' AND Student.id = Teaches.student AND Teaches.advisor = Scientist.id;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year FROM Scientist, Teaches, Scientist AS Advisor WHERE Advisor.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' AND Advisor.id = Teaches.advisor AND Teaches.student = Scientist.id;

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT Teaches.advisor AS grandparent, Teaches2.student AS grandchild FROM Teaches, Teaches AS Teaches2 WHERE Teaches.student = Teaches2.advisor;

Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.
Answer Match: read1(x); read2(x); read1(y); write1(x); write2(x); write1(y);

Q Text: Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
Answer Match: read1(x); read1(y); write1(x); read2(x); write1(y); commit1(x,y); write2(x); commit2(x);

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants(advisor, student) AS ( 	( -- seed --> should be a student of Ancestor (ret Ancestor, student) 		SELECT T.advisor, T.student 		FROM Teaches T 	) 	UNION 	 ( -- recu --> student of a descendant of Ancestor (ret Descendant, student ... I think) 		SELECT D.advisor, T.student 		FROM Descendants D, Teaches T 		WHERE D.student = T.advisor 	) ) SELECT advisor Ancestor, student Descendant FROM Descendants ;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE ErasmusDescendants(id) AS ( 	( -- seed (direct student of Erasmus) --> borrow from Q8 		SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year 		FROM Scientist S, Teaches T 		WHERE S.id = T.student  			AND T.advisor = (  				SELECT id 				FROM Scientist 				WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' 			) 	) 	UNION 	( -- recursive statement 		SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year 		FROM ErasmusDescendants ED, Scientist S, Teaches T 		WHERE ED.id = T.advisor -- S is student of an id (advisor) previously found in ED 			AND T.student = S.id 	) ) SELECT id, name, university, year FROM ErasmusDescendants;

Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT COUNT(Teaches.student), Scientist.name FROM Scientist INNER JOIN Teaches ON Teaches.advisor = Scientist.id GROUP BY Scientist.name ORDER BY COUNT(Teaches.student) desc;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year FROM Scientist INNER JOIN Teaches ON Teaches.student = Scientist.id WHERE Scientist.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year FROM Scientist INNER JOIN Teaches ON Teaches.student = Scientist.id WHERE Teaches.advisor = (SELECT DISTINCT Scientist.id FROM Scientist INNER JOIN Teaches ON Teaches.advisor = Scientist.id WHERE Scientist.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE ancestors(advisor) AS ( SELECT T.advisor, T.student FROM Teaches T UNION SELECT T.advisor, A.student FROM ancestors A, Teaches T WHERE A.advisor = T.student AND NOT T.advisor IS NULL) SELECT * FROM ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors(advisor, student) AS (    SELECT advisor, student  FROM Teaches  UNION   SELECT A.advisor, T.student  FROM Ancestors A  JOIN Teaches T ON A.student = T.advisor)    SELECT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT s.name, COUNT(t.student) AS num_students FROM Scientist s LEFT JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.advisor GROUP BY s.id, s.name ORDER BY num_students DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientist s JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.advisor JOIN Scientist e ON e.id = t.student WHERE e.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientist s JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.student JOIN Scientist e ON e.id = t.advisor WHERE e.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT t1.advisor AS grandparent, t3.student AS grandchild FROM Teaches t1 JOIN Teaches t2 ON t1.student = t2.advisor  -- t1's student is t2's advisor JOIN Teaches t3 ON t2.student = t3.advisor  -- t2's student is t3's advisor ;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestry AS (     -- base case: direct advisor-student relationships     SELECT advisor, student     FROM Teaches       UNION ALL       -- recursive case: extend by one generation each time     SELECT a.advisor, t.student     FROM Ancestry a     JOIN Teaches t ON a.student = t.advisor ) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM Ancestry;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (     -- base case find desiderius erasmus     SELECT id, name, university, year     FROM Scientist     WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'       UNION ALL       -- recursive case: find all direct students of each descendant     SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year     FROM Scientist s     JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.student     JOIN Descendants d ON t.advisor = d.id ) SELECT DISTINCT id, name, university, year FROM Descendants WHERE name != 'Desiderius Erasmus';  -- exclude erasmus from the final result

Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT Scientist.name, COUNT (Teaches.advisor) FROM Scientist, Teaches GROUP BY Scientist.name, Teaches.advisor ORDER BY Teaches.advisor DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S, Teaches T WHERE S.id = T.advisor AND university = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S, Teaches T WHERE S.id = T.student AND university = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors(advisor,student) AS (SELECT advisor, student FROM Teaches UNION SELECT Ancestors.advisor,Teaches.student FROM Ancestors,Teaches WHERE Ancestors.student=Teaches.advisor) SELECT * FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (SELECT Teaches.student FROM Teaches,Scientist WHERE Scientist.name='Desiderius Erasmus' AND Scientist.id=Teaches.advisor UNION SELECT Teaches.student FROM Descendants,Teaches WHERE Descendants.student=Teaches.advisor) SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE id IN (SELECT student FROM Descendants);

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors(advisor, student) AS (SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM Teaches UNION ALL SELECT a.advisor, t.student FROM Ancestors as a, Teaches as t WHERE a.student == t.advisor) SELECT DISTINCT * FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (     SELECT advisor, student     FROM Teaches     SELECT A.advisor, T.student     FROM Ancestors A     JOIN Teaches T ON A.student = T.advisor ) SELECT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.

Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
We want to prevent dirty reads by transaction T1, and we want to put minimal constraints on the concurrent execution of transactions T1 and T2. What isolation level(s) do we need for T1 and/or T2?
Answer: T1: Serializable and T2 Read Committed

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT*FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT*FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '% Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S1.* FROM Scientist S1, Scientist S2, Teaches T WHERE S1.id = T.advisor AND S2.id = T.student AND S2.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S2.* FROM Scientist S1, Scientist S2, Teaches T WHERE S1.id = T.advisor AND S2.id = T.student AND S1.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT T1.advisor, T2.student FROM Teaches T1, Teaches T2 WHERE T1.student = T2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestor (advisor, student) AS ( SELECT advisor, student from Teaches UNION SELECT T.advisor, A.student FROM Teaches T, Ancestor A WHERE T.student = A.advisor SELECT * FROM Ancestor;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestor (advisor, student) AS ( SELECT advisor, student from Teaches UNION SELECT T.advisor, A.student FROM Teaches T, Ancestor A WHERE T.student = A.advisor SELECT S2.* FROM Scientist S1, Scientist S2, Ancestor A WHERE S1.id = A.advisor AND S2.id = A.student AND S1.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientists s JOIN Advisors a ON s.id = a.advisor_id JOIN Scientists erasmus ON erasmus.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'  WHERE a.scientist_id = erasmus.id;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientists s JOIN Students st ON s.id = st.student_id JOIN Scientists erasmus ON erasmus.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' WHERE st.advisor_id = erasmus.id;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants(id, name, university, year) AS (     SELECT S.*     FROM Scientist S     JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.student     WHERE T.advisor = (         SELECT id         FROM Scientist         WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'     )     UNION       SELECT S.*     FROM Scientist S     JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.student     JOIN Descendants D ON T.advisor = D.id ) SELECT * FROM Descendants;

Q Text: Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.
Answer Match: write1(x); read2(x); write2(x); read1(x); write1(y); read1(y)

Q Text: Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
Answer Match: read1(x); read1(y); write1(x); read2(x); write2(x); commit2(x); write1(y); commit1(x, y)

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE University='Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE University LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT name, COUNT(student) FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE id=advisor GROUP BY name ORDER BY COUNT(student) DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT A.id, A.name, A.university, A.year FROM Scientist A JOIN Teaches T ON A.id = T.advisor WHERE T.student = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.student WHERE T.advisor = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT T1.advisor AS grandparent, T2.student AS grandchild FROM Teaches T1 JOIN Teaches T2 ON T1.student = T2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' UNION ALL SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientist s INNER JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.student INNER JOIN Descendants d ON t.advisor = d.id) SELECT DISTINCT id, name, university, year FROM Descendants;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
