Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT * id, name, university, year  FROM Scientist  WHERE University = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT * id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT name AS scientist_name, COUNT(student) AS num_students  FROM Scientist  JOIN Teaches ON Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor  GROUP BY Scientist.name  ORDER BY num_students DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year  FROM Scientist  JOIN Teaches ON Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor  JOIN Scientist AS Student ON Teaches.student = Student.id  WHERE Student.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT scientist.id, scientist.name FROM Scientist AS a JOIN Teaches ON a.id = teaches.advisor JOIN Scientist ON teaches.student = scientist.id WHERE a.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT t1.advisor AS grandparent, t2.student AS grandchild FROM Teaches AS t1 JOIN Teaches AS t2 ON t1.student = t2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (SELECT advisor, student FROM Teaches UNION ALL SELECT teaches.advisor, t2.student FROM Teaches  JOIN Ancestors t2 ON teaches.student = t2.advisor WHERE teaches.advisor != t2.student  ) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM Ancestors WHERE advisor != student;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS ( SELECT t.student AS id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Teaches t JOIN Scientist s ON t.advisor = s.id WHERE s.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' UNION ALL SELECT t.student AS id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Teaches t JOIN Scientist s ON t.advisor = s.id JOIN Descendants d ON t.advisor = d.id WHERE t.student != d.id  ) SELECT DISTINCT d.id, d.name, d.university, d.year FROM Descendants d ORDER BY d.id;

Q Text: Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Answer: T1: Serializable and T2 Read Committed

Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE Scientist.id = Teaches.student AND Teaches.advisor = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS ( SELECT  advisor AS ancestor, student AS descendant FROM Teaches UNION SELECT  a.ancestor AS ancestor, t.student AS descendant FROM Ancestors a, Teaches t WHERE a.descendant = t.advisor AND NOT a.ancestor IS NULL ) SELECT ancestor, descendant FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS ( SELECT t.student AS descendant FROM Teaches t, Scientist s WHERE t.advisor = s.id AND s.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' UNION SELECT t.student AS descendant FROM Ancestors a, Teaches t WHERE a.descendant = t.advisor AND NOT t.student IS NULL ) SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientist s WHERE s.id IN (SELECT descendant FROM Ancestors);

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT gp.advisor AS grandparent, gc.student AS grandchild FROM Teaches gp, Teaches gc WHERE gp.student = gc.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (     SELECT advisor, student     FROM Teaches     UNION     SELECT a.advisor, t.student     FROM Ancestors a, Teaches t     WHERE a.student = t.advisor ) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendant AS( SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' UNION  SELECT t.student, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Descendant d, Teaches t, Scientist s WHERE d.id = t.advisor AND t.student = s.id ) SELECT DISTINCT id, name, university, year FROM Descendant;

Q Text: Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.
Answer Match: read1(x); read2(x); write2(x); write1(x); read1(y); write1(y)

Q Text: Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
Answer Match: read1(x); write1(x); read2(x); write2(x); commit2(x); write1(y); commit1(x, y);

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. Answer: D SELECT id, name, university, year ┬╖ FROM Scientist ┬╖ WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: D SELECT id, name, university, year ┬╖ FROM Scientist ┬╖ WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: D SELECT S.name, COUNT(T.student) AS student_count ┬╖ FROM Scientist S ┬╖ LEFT JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.advisor ┬╖ GROUP BY S.name ┬╖ ORDER BY student_count DESC;

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: D SELECT DISTINCT T1.advisor AS grandparent, T2.student AS grandchild ┬╖ FROM Teaches T1 ┬╖ JOIN Teaches T2 ON T1.student = T2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: D WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS ( ┬╖     SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year ┬╖     FROM Scientist S ┬╖     WHERE S.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' ┬╖     UNION ALL ┬╖     SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year ┬╖     FROM Descendants D ┬╖     JOIN Teaches T ON D.id = T.advisor ┬╖     JOIN Scientist S ON T.student = S.id ┬╖ ) ┬╖ SELECT DISTINCT id, name, university, year ┬╖ FROM Descendants;

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT S.name, COUNT(T.student) AS student_count FROM Scientist S LEFT JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.advisor GROUP BY S.name ORDER BY student_count DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (     SELECT advisor, student     FROM Teaches     UNION ALL     SELECT T1.advisor, T2.student     FROM Teaches T1     JOIN Ancestors A ON T1.student = A.advisor     JOIN Teaches T2 ON T2.advisor = A.student ) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year FROM Scientist, Teaches, Scientist AS s WHERE s.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' AND s.id = Teaches.advisor AND Teaches.student = Scientist.id;

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT t1.advisor AS grandparent, t2.student AS grandchild FROM Teaches t1, Teaches t2 WHERE t1.student = t2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE AdvisorHierarchy AS (SELECT advisor, student FROM Teaches UNION SELECT AdvisorHierarchy.advisor, Teaches.student FROM AdvisorHierarchy, Teaches WHERE AdvisorHierarchy.student = Teaches.advisor) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM AdvisorHierarchy;

Answer Match: read1(x); read1(y); write2(x); write1(y); read2(x); write1(x)

Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT Scientist.name, COUNT(Teaches.student) AS student_count FROM Scientist JOIN Teaches ON Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor GROUP BY Scientist.name ORDER BY student_count DESC;

Answer: SELECT *FROM Scientist JOIN Teaches ON Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor WHERE Teaches.student = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (     -- Base Case: Select the direct advisor-student relationship     SELECT advisor, student     FROM Teaches     UNION ALL     -- Recursive Case: Find advisors of the advisor (i.e., ancestors)     SELECT t1.advisor, t2.student     FROM Teaches t1     JOIN Ancestors t2 ON t1.student = t2.advisor ) -- Final Selection: Select all unique (advisor, student) pairs from the recursive result SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (     -- Base case: Select Desiderius Erasmus     SELECT id, name, university, year     FROM Scientist     WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'          UNION ALL          -- Recursive case: Find students of the current descendants     SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year     FROM Scientist s     JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.student     JOIN Descendants d ON t.advisor = d.id ) -- Final selection: Get all descendants of Desiderius Erasmus SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Descendants WHERE name != 'Desiderius Erasmus';  -- Exclude Erasmus himself

Answer: with recursive Ancestors(ancestor, student) as (select T1.advisor as ancestor, T2.student as student from Teaches T1, Teaches T2 where T1.advisor = T2.student union select T1.advisor as ancestor, T2.student as student from Ancestors A, Teaches T1, Teaches T2 where A.ancestor = T1.advisor and T1.advisor = T2.student and not T1.advisor is null) select * from Ancestors;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestor AS ( SELECT advisor, student FROM Teaches UNION SELECT Teaches.advisor, Ancestor.student FROM Teaches, Ancestor WHERE Teaches.student = Ancestor.advisor ) SELECT advisor, student FROM Ancestor;

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT s.name, COUNT(t.student) as supervised_students FROM Scientist s LEFT JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.advisor GROUP BY s.id, s.name ORDER BY supervised_students DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientist s JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.advisor JOIN Scientist e ON t.student = e.id WHERE e.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientist s JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.student JOIN Scientist e ON t.advisor = e.id WHERE e.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT t1.advisor AS grandparent, t2.student AS grandchild FROM Teaches t1 JOIN Teaches t2 ON t1.student = t2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (     SELECT advisor, student     FROM Teaches       UNION ALL       SELECT t1.advisor, t2.student     FROM Teaches t1     JOIN Ancestors t2 ON t1.student = t2.advisor ) SELECT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (     SELECT id, name, university, year     FROM Scientist     WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'       UNION ALL       SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year     FROM Scientist s     JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.student     JOIN Descendants d ON t.advisor = d.id ) SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Descendants WHERE name != 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.
Answer Match: write2(x), read1(x), read1(y), write1(x), write1(y), read2(x)

Q Text: Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
Answer Match: read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y); read2(x);  write2(x); commit1(x,y),  commit2(x)

Answer: T1 Read Committed; T2 Read Uncommitted

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year FROM Scientist WHERE  Scientist.university= 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year FROM Scientist WHERE  Scientist.university LIKE  '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT Scientist.name, COUNT(Teaches.student) FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor GROUP BY Scientist.name ORDER BY COUNT(Teaches.student) DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor AND Teaches.student  = (SELECT Scientist.id FROM Scientist WHERE Scientist.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE Scientist.id = Teaches.student AND Teaches.advisor  = (SELECT Scientist.id FROM Scientist WHERE Scientist.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT T1.advisor AS grandparent, T2.student AS grandchild FROM Teaches T1 JOIN Teaches T2 ON T1.student = T2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors(advisor, student) AS (     SELECT advisor, student     FROM Teaches     UNION     SELECT A.advisor, T.student     FROM Ancestors A     JOIN Teaches T     ON A.student = T.advisor ) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT Scientist.name, COUNT(student) AS num_students FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor GROUP BY Scientist.name ORDER BY num_students DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor AND student = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year  FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor AND advisor = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT t1.advisor AS grandparent, t2.student AS grandchild FROM Teaches AS t1, Teaches AS t2, Scientist AS s1, Scientist AS s2 WHERE t1.student = t2.advisor AND t1.advisor = s1.id AND t2.student = s2.id AND SUBSTRING_INDEX(s1.name, ' ', -1) = SUBSTRING_INDEX(s2.name, ' ', -1);

Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.
Answer Match: read1(x); read2(x); write2(x); write1(x); read1(y); write1(y)

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: FROM Scientist SELECT id, name, university, year WHERE Scientist.university == 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT S.name AS scientist_name, COUNT(T.student) AS num_students FROM Scientist S LEFT JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.advisor GROUP BY S.id, S.name ORDER BY num_students DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.advisor WHERE T.student = ( SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' );

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.student WHERE T.advisor = (     SELECT id     FROM Scientist     WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' );

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT T1.advisor AS grandparent, T2.student AS grandchild FROM Teaches T1 JOIN Teaches T2 ON T1.student = T2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (     SELECT advisor, student     FROM Teaches     UNION       SELECT A.advisor, T.student     FROM Ancestors A     JOIN Teaches T ON A.student = T.advisor ) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (     -- Base Case: Direct students of Desiderius Erasmus     SELECT T.student     FROM Teaches T     WHERE T.advisor = (         SELECT id         FROM Scientist         WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'     )     UNION     -- Recursive Case: Students of students (descendants)     SELECT T.student     FROM Teaches T     JOIN Descendants D ON T.advisor = D.student ) SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S WHERE S.id IN (SELECT student FROM Descendants);

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT name, COUNT(*) FROM Scientist INNER JOIN Teaches ON Teaches.advisor=Scientist.id GROUP BY name ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist INNER JOIN Teaches ON Teaches.advisor=Scientist.id WHERE Teaches.student=(SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name='Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist INNER JOIN Teaches ON Teaches.student=Scientist.id WHERE Teaches.advisor=(SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name='Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT T1.advisor, T2.student FROM Teaches T1 JOIN Teaches T2 ON T1.student = T2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (SELECT advisor, student FROM Teaches UNION SELECT t.advisor, t2.student FROM Teaches t JOIN Ancestors t2 ON t.student = t2.advisor) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (SELECT advisor, student FROM Teaches UNION SELECT t.advisor, t2.student FROM Teaches t JOIN Ancestors t2 ON t.student = t2.advisor) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM Ancestors WHERE advisor=(SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name='Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.
Answer Match: read1(x); write2(x); read1(y); write1(x); read2(x); write1(y);

Q Text: Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
Answer Match: read1(x); read1(y); write2(x); write1(x); read2(x); commit2(x); write1(y); commit1(x,y);

Q Text: We want to prevent dirty reads by transaction T1, and we want to put minimal constraints on the concurrent execution of transactions T1 and T2. What isolation level(s) do we need for T1 and/or T2?
Answer: T1: Serializable and T2 Read Committed

Q Text: We want to prevent dirty reads by transaction T1, and we want to put minimal constraints on the concurrent execution of transactions T1 and T2. What isolation level(s) do we need for T1 and/or T2?
Answer: T1 Read Committed; T2 Read Uncommitted

The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT Scientist.name, COUNT(Teaches.student) AS nr_of_students FROM Scientist LEFT JOIN Teaches ON Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor GROUP BY Scientist.id, Scientist.name ORDER BY nr_of_students DESC;

Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist JOIN Teaches ON Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor WHERE Teaches.student = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Answer: SELECT name, count(student) FROM Scientist s, Teaches t WHERE s.id = t.advisor GROUP BY name ORDER BY count(student) DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s1.id, s1.name, s1.university, s1.year FROM Scientist s1, Scientist s2 , Teaches WHERE s1.id = advisor AND s2.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' AND s2.id = student;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s1.id, s1.name, s1.university, s1.year FROM Scientist s1, Scientist s2 , Teaches WHERE s1.id = student AND s2.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' AND s2.id = advisor;

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT t1.advisor, t2.student FROM Teaches t1, Teaches t2 WHERE t1.student = t2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Pair(advisor, student) AS  ( SELECT advisor, student  FROM Teaches UNION SELECT p.advisor, t.student FROM Pair p, Teaches t WHERE p.student = t.advisor ) SELECT * FROM Pair;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Pair(id, name, university, year) AS  ( SELECT s2.id, s2.name, s2.university, s2.year FROM Teaches, Scientist s1 , Scientist s2 WHERE s1.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' AND s1.id = advisor  AND s2.id = student UNION SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Pair p, Scientist s, Teaches WHERE p.id = advisor AND s.id = student ) SELECT * FROM Pair;

Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE AncestorPath AS(SELECT advisor, student FROM Teaches UNION ALL SELECT T1.advisor, T2.student FROM Teaches T1 JOIN AncestorPath T2 ON T1.student = T2.advisor WHERE T1.advisor !=T2.student) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM AncestorPath WHERE advisor != student;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.student WHERE T.advisor = ( SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' ) UNION ALL SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.student JOIN Descendants D ON T.advisor = D.id WHERE S.id NOT IN (SELECT id FROM Descendants)) SELECT DISTINCT id, name, university, year FROM Descendants;
